title: Trump Reboots Wind War as Energy Bills Soar
description: President Trump vows to stop all wind projects, paying $1 billion to halt East Coast offshore farms while energy demands from AI and EVs skyrocket.
lede: President Trump is aggressively dismantling America's offshore wind industry, delivering on campaign promises even as domestic energy demand surges. His administration just handed a French energy firm nearly $1 billion to abandon two major East Coast wind projects, signaling a definitive pivot back to fossil fuels.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: President Trump explicitly laid out his energy agenda at a recent White House event with Interior Secretary Doug Burgum. "My goal is to not let any windmill be built. They're losers," Trump declared, claiming his administration has not approved a single wind project since taking office.

Acting on these orders, Burgum's Interior Department struck a nearly $1 billion deal with French energy giant TotalEnergies. The unprecedented agreement pays the company to forfeit federal leases for offshore wind farms near New York and North Carolina, with the funds redirected to a liquefied natural gas plant in Texas.

The administration also issued highly unusual stop-work orders for five offshore wind power projects already under construction off the coasts of Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, New York, and Virginia. These projects represented billions of dollars in investment and tens of thousands of jobs.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The timing of the administration's crackdown is critical. Energy demand is rising sharply nationwide, driven largely by the massive power requirements of data centers, artificial intelligence, cryptocurrency mining, and electric vehicles.

The five East Coast projects alone were designed to produce enough energy to power 2.5 million homes. Halting construction mid-stream has sent shockwaves through the energy sector. Neil Chatterjee, a former Federal Energy Regulatory Commission chairman during Trump's first term, called the move problematic, warning it "sets a really, really bad precedent that can be used by future administrations to stop fossil fuel projects."

The Natural Resources Defense Council labeled the administration's actions "unprecedented." By aggressively targeting wind power, the administration is prioritizing a partisan passion project over market-driven energy diversification.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Trump's hostility toward wind energy is not new; it dates back to at least 2012 when he fought an offshore wind farm near his golf course in Aberdeen, Scotland. He frequently cites unverified claims about noise and bird deaths, ignoring data from the National Audubon Society showing that outdoor cats and building collisions kill significantly more birds than turbines.

While his feelings haven't changed, his power to enforce them has. The White House defends the policy shift, with spokesperson Taylor Rogers asserting that Trump's "energy dominance agenda is unleashing reliable, affordable, secure energy sources."

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: The administration's efforts have hit significant legal roadblocks. Energy companies quickly sued over the stop-work orders, and federal judges have rejected the administration's arguments, granting preliminary injunctions that allowed construction to resume. One of the embattled projects is already delivering power.

However, long-planned future projects remain stalled, awaiting federal approvals that are unlikely to materialize under the current Interior Department. The ongoing legal and regulatory battles will likely slow the industry's growth, potentially making the U.S. less competitive in the global clean energy market while doing little to alleviate rising consumer energy costs.
